text: GONNEVILLE, a village of France, in the dep. of La-Manche, 5 m. E of Cherbourg. Pop. 1,370. There are cotton yarn spinning-mills here.—Also a com. in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Criquetot-Lesneval. Pop. 745.
GONNINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Schwarzwald circle, 7 m. SE of Tubingen, at the foot of the Rossberg. Pop. 1,840.
GONNORD, a town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, 4 m. NNW of Vihiers, on the Gué- Gautier.
GONNOSFANADIGA, a commune and village of Sardinia, 30 m. NW of Cagliari. Pop. 2,925.
GONOWITZ, a small town of Styria, 50 m. S of Gratz, and 11 m. NE of Cilli. Pop. 604. Lead, iron, and coal, are mined in the vicinity.
GONPOT, a town on the N coast of Mindanao, 45 m. WNW of Butuan.
GONRIEUX, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, cant. and 3 m. WSW of Couvin. Pop. 500.
GONSALVO (SAINT). See GONÇALO.
GONSAWA, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, reg. and 30 m. SSW of Bromberg. Pop. 350.
GONSENHEIM, a large village of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the cant. and 7 m. N of Niederolm.
GONTAIR. See GUNTUR.
GONTAUT, a town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, 8 m. ESE of Marmande. Pop. 1,100.
GONTHEY. See CONTHEY.
GONZAGA, a small town of Austrian Lombardy, 16 m. S of Mantua.
GONZALES (SAN), a point of land forming the SE extremity of Vancouver's island, in N lat. 48° 25', W long. 123° 19'.

GOOBANG, a river, or chain of creeks, in SE. Australia, having its sources in the ravines between Harvey's and Croker's ranges, and flowing towards the Lachlan, which it joins in S lat. 33° 5′ 20″, E long. 147° 13′ 10″.
GOOBER. See GOBAR.

GOOCHLAND, a county in the E of Virginia, U. S., surrounded by Louisa, Fluvanna, Henrico, Hanover, and Powhatan cos. Area 300 sq. m. Coal and gold occur in this co. Pop. in 1816, 10,203; in 1841, 9,760.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated 1 m. N of James river, and 30 m. WNW of Richmond.
GOOCHNAUTH, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, on the Bunass river, 3 m. SE of Rahdunpore. The country in its vicinity is fertile and highly cultivated.
GOODESTONE, or Goodson, a parish of Norfolk, 35 m. ENE of Stoke-Ferry. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 500.
GOOD FORTUNE. See PORA.

GOOD FRIDAY HARBOUR, an excellent port at the NE point of Rat island, in the Easter group, on the W coast of Australia, in S lat. 32° 42′ 50″, long. 1° 57′ 50" W of Swan river. It has a general depth of from 15 to 17 fath., but is full of coral knolls, which necessitate the utmost vigilance.
GOOD HOPE. See GOEDE HOOP.
GOOD HOPE, a township in Hocking co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. 469.—Also a township in Walton co., in Georgia.—Also a township in Noble co., in Ohio. G00
GOOD HOPE (FORT), a station of the Hudson's Bay company, about 120 m. below the confluence of the Mackenzie and Great Bear river.
GOOD'S ISLAND, an island on the N coast of New Holland, in Torres strait, in S lat. 10° 32'. It is about 2 m. in length, hilly, covered with wood, and of a rocky soil.
GOODLEIGH, a parish of Devon, 3 m. ENE of Barnstaple. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. in 1831, 442; in 1841, 335.

GOODNESTON, a parish in Kent, 23 m. SSE of Wingham. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. in 1831, 486; in 1841, 424.—Also a parish in the same co., 2 m. E of Faversham. Area 140 acres. Pop. in 1831, 74; in 1841, 60.
GOODRICH. See GODERICH.

GOODWIN SANDS, a range of sandbanks, near the isle of Thanet, extending about 10 m. along the coast of Kent, between the N. and S. Forelands, and on a parallel line with Deal. "Whatsoever old wives tell of Goodwyne, earle of Kent, in time of Edward the Confessour, and his sandes, it appeareth," says Lambard, "by Hector Boetius, the Brittish chronicler, that theise sands weare mayne land, and some tyme of the possession of Earl Godwyne, and by a great inundation of the sea, they weare taken therfroe, at which tyme also much harme was done in Scotland and Flanders by the same rage of the water, for Sylvester Gyrald reporteth, that Hen, I. sent dyvers Flemings, which weare dryven out of their countrye by the furye of the sea into the north partes about Carleol, which weare afterwards removed into Wales." These lands are said to have been inundated in the year 1100, and to have previously extended to upwards of 4,000 acres of low grounds, which were defended from the sea by a great wall, requiring constant care in its preservation. The tract was ultimately given to St. Austin's monastery near Canterbury, the abbot of which neglected the wall; and, in consequence, the sea broke in and overwhelmed the whole, converting it into those famous, or more properly infamous sands, which are so dangerous to vessels riding in the Downs, though by breaking the waves that would otherwise in E winds roll in upon the shore with immense impetuosity, they contribute to the safety of that celebrated roadstead. They are divided by a narrow swatch or channel navigable only by small boats, into two parts; and are in many places dry at low water. Their position, however, is often shifted by the force of the tides, aided by violent storms. The material of which they are composed is soft, porous, and tenacious. When the water is off, these banks become hard and firm; but the moment the tide begins to cover them they again become soft, and shift to and fro with the waves, occasioning that redness of the water which is plainly discoverable from the town of Deal. In hazy weather, a bell is kept constantly ringing to warn the approaching mariner of his danger; and floating-light vessels are stationed near them. An iron beacon erected upon them in 1846 was swept away in 1847.

GOOLE, a township and inland port in the p. of Snaith, W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SSW of Howden, on the W bank of the Ouse, at its junction with Dutch river, and about 10 m. above the junction of the Ouse with the Trent. Area 3,020 acres. Pop. in 1801, 294; in 1831, 1,671; in 1841, 2,850. The town of Goole has recently been formed into a port, with a custom-house and large warehouses, including bonded warehouses. There are two docks constructed by the Aire and Calder Navigation company at an expense of £200,000. One of these, the ship-dock, is 600 ft. long and 20 ft. wide, with 18 ft. depth of water; and will contain 54 sail of square-rigged vessels, of which number the quay admits 17 to load and unload at once. The other, the barge-dock, for coasting-vessels, is 900 ft. long by 150 ft. wide, with 8 ft. water. It is calculated to contain 200 sail of the river-craft which navigate into the interior of the country by means of the canal which passes W from this dock, and forms an uninterrupted communication by water between the German ocean and the Irish sea. Between the docks and the river is a basin or entrance-harbour 250 ft. long, by 200 ft. wide, with 9 ft. water. It has two entrance-gates, and interior gates opening upon the docks. Although G. is situated so far inland, vessels drawing from 15 to 17 ft. water have arrived and discharged their cargoes here in perfect safety; and the port is likely to become an important station for inland and maritime trade. The gross receipt of customs' duty, in 1838, was £60,066; in 1839, £79,003; in 1846, £44,920. There were 121,445 tons of coals shipped hence in 1835. Corn from Lincolnshire, barley from Norfolk and Suffolk, and wool from the Eastern counties, are also largely imported; but since the opening of the Hull and Selby railway, the foreign commerce has considerably declined. G. is now connected by railway with Pontefract and Wakefield; and at the latter place this railway joins the Manchester and Leeds line.
GOOR, a small town of Holland, in the prov. of Overyssel, on the Regge, 28 m. SE of Zwolle.
GOOS, or Gos, a small village of Nubia, near the junction of the Nile and the Tacazze, in N lat. 17° 58'.

GOOSE CREEK, a river of New Jersey, which runs into the Atlantic in N lat. 39° 53'.—Also a river of Kentucky, which runs into the Ohio, in N lat. 38° 10'.—Also a river of N. Carolina, which runs into the Atlantic, in N lat. 34° 40'.—Also a river of Virginia, which runs E, and enters the Roanoke after a course of 30 m.

GOOSE ISLAND, an island in the river St. Lawrence, about 12 m. below the island of Orleans. It is connected with Crane island by means of a marsh, and together they are about 12 m. in length. They are both well-cultivated.—Also an island in Christmas sound, on the S coast of Tierra-del-Fuego. -Also a small island lying off the S coast of New Holland, which gives name to a commodious bay, in S lat. 34° 5′ 23".—Also an island in Bass strait, in S lat. 40° 18′ 41″, E long. 147° 48′ 50″.
GOOSE LAKE, a lake of North America, in N lat. 52° 40′, W long. 95° 50′.—Also a lake of North America, in the Knistineaux territory, in N lat. 53° 25', W long. 102° 30'.
GOOSE POND, a township in Oglethorpe co., in the state of Georgia, U.S., 101 m. N of Milledgeville.
GOOSE RIVER, a river of America, which runs into the Mississippi in N lat. 43° 14'.—Also a river of S. Carolina, which runs into Cooper's river in N lat. 33° 2'.
GOOSEBERRY ISLANDS, a group of small islands near the E coast of Newfoundland, 24 m. NW of Cape Bonavista, in N lat. 49° 10'.
GOOSEBERRY ROCKS, a group of islets off the coast of Essex co., Massachusetts, U. S., 2 m. N of Marble-head, and 21 m. NE of Boston.
GOOSNARGH, a township in the p. of Kirkham, in Lancashire, 5 m. NE of Preston. Pop. 1,621.
GOOSTREY, a chapelry in the p. of Sandbach, in Cheshire, 7 m. NNE of Sandbach. Area 1,230 acres. Pop., with Barnshaw, 325.
GOPALAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the district and 33 m. NW of Benares.
GOPALGOR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwah, 12 m. N of Serondji.
GOPALGUNGE, a town of Hindostan, 93 m. ENE of Calcutta, on the r. bank of the Mudametti.
GOPALPILLY, a town of Hindostan, in the district and 27 m. N of Vizagapatam.
GOPALPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, district and 30 m. S of Gorakpore, on the 1. bank of the Ganges, 21 m. NE of Azimghur.— Also a town in the prov. and 111 m. SSW of Agra.
GOPAMOW, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, district of Kairabad, on the W side of the Gumti, 63 m. NW of Lucknau. It formerly possessed a Mahommedan college of considerable repute.

GOPPINGEN, a neat town of Wurtemberg, in the Danube circle, on the r. bank of the Fils, 27 m. NW of Ulm. Pop. 5,035. It has manufactories of woollen-stuffs, silk-ribbons, pottery-ware, and paper, and an active trade in wool, with large sheep-fairs. In 1782, it was almost entirely burned down. In in consequence. GOR the neighbourhood are the ruins of the ancient castle of Hohenstauffen, the original residence of the line of Suabian princes, which gave emperors to Germany in the 12th cent. There are mineral springs in the vicinity.

GOPPLO, or GOPPLEN, a lake of Prussian Poland, in the reg. of Bromberg, circle of Inowraclaw, formerly of great extent, but now in great part dried up. It is still 22 m. long, and 2 m. broad; and supplies the Netz with a great part of its water.
GOR, a small town of Farsistan, in Persia, 57 m. NW of Schiraz.—Also a town of Spain, in Andalusia, 43 m. ENE of Grenada. Pop. 1,600.
GORA, a town of Poland, in the obw. and 20 m. SSE of Warsaw, on the Vistula.—Also a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Posen, reg. and 22 m. SSW of Bromberg.
GORABUNDER, a village and fort of Hindostan, at the N extremity of the island of Salsette, 20 m. N of Bombay.
GORAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, 27 m. SW of Ivar, near the r. bank of the Beyturnah.

GORALES, a tribe of Poles inhabiting the mountains of Gallicia, on the frontiers of Hungary, from Dulko to Stanislawou. They are distinguished by marked outlines from the inhabitants of the low country. They are generally supposed to be descended from the Sauromates, a tribe who retreated into Europe before the arms of Mithridates, about 80 B. C.

GORAM, a small island of the Molucca group, about one day's sail E by N from Banda, in S lat. 4° 3'. It is nearly 21 m. in circuit, and with the neighbouring islands of Manovolko and Salawatta, forms what is known as the G. group. These islands are high and fertile. Rice is grown on the main island, but its cultivation is regarded as the exclusive hereditary right of the people of G. The forests afford hogs; and fowls and goats are numerous. The inhabitants manufacture a dark red cotton stuff, with cross stripes of blue, which is much in demand at the Ki and Arru islands. The yarn for this manufacture is chiefly obtained from Bali. They also manufacture broad klewangs, or hangers, from 2 to 3 ft. long, and beautifully flowered on the blade, which are considered superior in temper to those made in Java.
GORASEDA, or Gorsda, a town of Turkey, in Bosnia, 32 m. SE of Bosna-Serai, on the l. bank of the Drin. Pop. 1,200.
GORAY, a town of Poland, in the obwod and 27 m. W of Zamosk, on the Lada.
GORBALS. See GLASGOW.
GORBATA, a town in the S part of the regency of Tunis, perhaps the ancient Orbita, 12 m. SW of Gafsa.

GORBATOF, a town of European Russia, in the gov. of Nizni-Novgorod, on the Oker, a little below the confluence of the Kliasma, 41 m. SW of Nizni- Novgorod. Pop. 2,070. The surrounding country is fertile in grain, lint, and hemp.
GORBITZ (NIEDER), a town of Saxony, in the circle and 4 m. WSW of Dresden. Pop. 1,500.

GORBITZKOI, a fort of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, at the confluence of the Chilka and the Little Gorbitza. GORCAH, GORCHA, or GORKHA, a rajaship or district of Nepal, situated between the 28th and 29th parallels, in the fork between the Marichangdi and Trisulganga. It is a mountainous barren country, intersected by numerous hill streams.-Its capital, of the same name, is situated on the summit of a lofty hill, in N lat. 27° 52', E long. 84° 22'. Pop. 10,000. After the conquest of Nepal in 1768, the seat of government was transferred to Catmandu, and the city of G. has fallen into comparative decay

GORCE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. and 5 m. NNE of Vallon. Pop. 1,200.—Also a v. in the dep. of Gironde, cant. and 4 m. NE of Guitre, near the r. bank of the Dronne. Pop. 1,500.
GORCHEN, or GORKA, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and 52 m. S of Posen. Pop. 1,500.

GORCUM, or GORINCHEM, a small town of Holland, in the prov. of S. Holland, on the r. bank of the Maas, at the influx of the Linge, 22 m. ESE of Rotterdam. Pop. 8,199. It is fortified, and made some resistance at the time of the Prussian invasion of Holland in 1787; it was again the scene of fighting in the spring of 1814, the French on their retreat having left a garrison in it. Its trade, compared with that of most Dutch towns, is inconsiderable; but it has extensive fisheries, particularly of salmon, and manufactories of tobacco-pipes.
GORDA (CAPE), a cape on the NW coast of Mindanao, in S lat. 8° 5'.

GORDA (Punta), a cape on the S coast of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 22°, 54 m. S of Havana.- Also a cape on the coast of Brazil, in S lat. 16° 36'. -Also a cape on the Mosquito shore, in N lat. 14° 28', 150 m. S of Belize. There is a Carib settlement here with about 500 inhabitants.—Also a cape on the coast of Bolivia, in S lat. 19° 19′, W long. 70° 19½′. -Also a low jutting prong on the coast of Chili, 7 m. S of the Quebrada-de-Camarones.—Also a cape on the coast of Mexico, in N lat. 19° 14' 52", W long. 96° 10′ 30″.
GORDALIZA - DE - LA - LOMA, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Valladolid, 18 m. ENE of Villalpandos.
GORDALIZA-DEL-PENO, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 27 m. SE of Leon. Pop. 300.
GORDES, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, 9 m. WNW of Apt. Pop. of town and com. 2,848 in 1841; of cant., comprising 8 coms., 7,390.
GORDJDI, or GARSDEN, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 36 m. WSW of Telsh, on the r. bank of the Minge.
GORDO (CABO), a cape of Costa Rica, on the NW coast of the Golfo Dulce, in N lat. 8° 23'.
GORDOM, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 165 m. NW of Wilna, on the Tenne.

GORDON, a parish and village of Scotland, in the co. of Berwick, 8 m. NW of Kelso. Pop. in 1831, 882; in 1841, 903. The p. is distinguished for giving title to the ducal family of Gordon. They are supposed to have settled within its limits in the reign of Malcolm Canmore; and when they removed to the N, they not only transferred some of its local names to the territories or objects of their new home, but afterwards recurred to it for their ducal title. Huntly-which, through the medium of the N domain named after it, gave them their titles successively of Lord, Earl, and Marquis—was a village in the W extremity of G. parish; and, though commemorated only by a solitary tree which marks its site, survived till a recent date in the form of a small hamlet.
GORDON (CAPE), a headland on Joinville island, in the S. Shetland group, in S lat. 63° 48', W long. 57° 21'.
GORDON (POINT), a cape on the S coast of one of the islands in Broughton's archipelago, in N lat. 50° 48'.
GORDONA, a mountain in the N of Lombardy, in the prov. of Como, valley of Intelvi, 4 m. S of Fidele. Alt. 4,540 ft.
GORDON'S ISLAND, a small island at the mouth of the Mississippi, in the S pass, with a lighthouse upon it in N lat. 28° 59' 17", W long. 89° 7' 24".
GORDON'S RIVER, a river of Van Diemen's GOR Land, which empties itself into the S part of Macquarie harbour, 3 or 4 m. E of Birch's river.

GORE, a district of Upper Canada, consisting of the cos. of Wentworth and Halton, and containing some of the best cultivated townships in Canada. It is intersected by the Grand river. Its cap. is Hamilton, with a pop. in 1848 of 9,889. Dundas and Galt are large and rapidly increasing villages. Pop. in 1832, 27,224; in 1841, 31,507; in 1848, 59,015; of whom 4,493 were returned as proprietors; 5,066 as engaged in agriculture; and 1,653 in trade or commerce. Cultivated acres in 1846, 266,842.
GORE (POINT), a cape on the W coast of North America, in N lat. 49° 20'.
GORE'S BAY, a bay on the SE coast of the Middle island of New Zealand, in S lat. 43° 12'.— Also a bay on the coast of Arctic America, in N lat. 66° 12′ 23″, W long. 84° 30′ 05″.

GORE ISLAND, an island in the N. Pacific ocean, so named by Cook in 1778. This island, which has its N point in N lat. 60° 18', W long. 172° 4', is narrow, especially at the low necks of land which connect the hills of which it is composed. It is about 30 m. in length from NW to SE.
GOREE. See GOEDEREEDE.

GORE'E, a small island off the Senegal coast of Africa, little more than 1 m. from the S shore of the promontory which forms Cape Verd, in N lat. 14° 39' 55", W long. 17° 26' 40". It is a mere rock, 400 fath. in length, and 167 fath. in extreme breadth. It produces nothing; and its importance is solely derived from its inaccessible situation, which renders it capable of being converted into an important military position. The first European power which occupied this spot was the Dutch, in 1617. They strengthened it by several fortifications. It was taken, in 1663, by Admiral Holmes, and retaken two years after by De-Ruyter. In 1677, a French squadron laid siege to it, and forced it to surrender in a short time; and by the treaty of Nimeguen, it was ceded to France in 1678. In 1809 it was taken by the British, but was restored with Senegal in 1816. The E and S sides of this island are very steep. The N side is the most accessible. The French government has greatly strengthened its military defences.—On a sandy plain, at the foot of the rock, on the N side of the island, is the town of G., supposed to contain about 5,000 inhabitants. It is the entrepot for all the French trade with the opposite coast of Africa.
GORELOI, one of the Alentian chain of islands, in N lat. 52°, E long. 178° 3'.
GORESBRIDGE, a village of co. Kilkenny, p. of Grange-Silvia, 23 m. E of Gowran. Pop. 921.
GOREY, a parish and town in co. Wexford. Area of p. 5,314 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,387; in 1841, 4,896. The town is 51 m. NE of Camolin. Pop. 3,365.
GORGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. N of Alicant. Pop. 790.
GORGENY, or St. Emrich, a small town of Transylvania, in the co. of Thorenburg, 21 m. NE of Neumarkt, on the r. bank of the Gorgeny.
GORGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Clisson. Pop. 1,598.— Also a com. in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Périers. Pop. 1,232.
GORGO, a village of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 19 m. NE of Treviso. Pop. 1,020.
GORGOGLIONE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 30 m. SW of Matera. Pop. 1,100.
GORGON (Sr.), a village of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. and 7 m. WNW of Mont-Benoit. Pop. 206.

GORGONA, a small island in the Pacific ocean, about 18 m. from the coast of Peru, in N lat. 2° 57', W long. 78° 25'. It is about 6 m. long, and 1 m. broad, and is desert and uncultivated. - Also an island in the Mediterranean, between Corsica and Leghorn, in N lat. 43° 26', 24½ m. N by E of Capraia, belonging to Tuscany. Pop. 70. It is 9 m. in circuit.-Also a town of New Granada, 20 m. NW of Panama, on the coast of Chagres, at its highest navigable point, and on the line of the railway now forming across the isthmus which, it is expected, will be open from Navy bay to this place about 27 m. in January 1852, when the remainder of the line to the Pacific, a distance of about 19 m., will be passable for locomotives in 1852.
GORGONILLA, a small island of the Pacific, near the coast of Ecuador, in N lat. 1° 47'.
GORGONZOLA, a town of Lombardy, in the deleg. and 12 m. ENE of Milan. Pop. 2,310. It conducts an active trade in Strachino cheese, by the Martesana canal.
GORGUE (LA), a small town of France, in the dep. of Nord, 12 m. W of Lille, on the r. bank of the Lys. Pop. 3,233. It has linen manufactures.

GORHAM, a township in the co. of Cumberland, state of Maine, U. S., 60 m. SW of Augusta. Pop. 3,001.—Also a township in the co. of Ontario, New York, 197 m. W of Albany. Pop. 2,779.—Also a township in Coos co., in New Hampshire, 197 m. N of Concord. Pop. 156.—Also a township in Lucas co., in Ohio, on Tiffin's river. Pop. 343.

GORI, a town of Russian Georgia, in the district of Kartli, on the Kur, 43 m. NW of Tiflis. Pop. 3,000. It is defended by a fortress on the l. bank of the Didi-Liakhei, which here joins the Kur.—Also a town of Russia, in the gov. and 45 m. NE of Mohilev.
GORIANO-DELLE-VALLI, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo-Ultra 2da, 3 m. W of Acciano. Pop. 600.

GORIN, a river of Russian Poland, in the govs. of Velhynia and Minsk, which rises 18 m. SW of Kremenetz; and joins the Pripet or Pripatz, after a N course of about 260 m., in which it passes Zaslov. It receives the Slutch on the r. bank, and the Vallia and Ostvitza on the l.
GORING, a parish of Oxfordshire, 12 m. W of Henley-on-Thames. Area 4,670 acres. Pop. 971.— Also a p. of Sussex, 3 m. W of Worthing. Area 2,120 aeres. Pop. 50.

GORISSEIFFEN, a village of Prussian Silesia, in the reg. and 30 m. WSW of Liegnitz. Pop. 2,082. GORITZ, GORICE, GORIZIA, or GÖRZ, a prov. of Austria, situated in the Litorale or coast-land, and bounded on the NE and S by the duchy of Carniola, and on the W by the Venetian states. It was constituted in 1817 a circle of the new kingdom of Illyria. It is divided into the three districts of Canale, Grandiska, and Goritz. On the N side it extends among the Alps; around the town of G., and throughout the SW districts it produces vines and the fruits common in Italy. It has the title of Contéa principesca, or 'Princely county,' having, like the Tyrol, been for many ages under the immediate sovereignty of the princes of the house of Austria. As it is the smallest among the possessions of that dynasty, so is it the last enumerated in the emperor's titles. To the ancient territory of the county, a part of the Venetian province of Friuli (Monfalcone), and also a small part of Carniola (Duino) has been annexed, Aquileia remaining in the territory. The whole district contained in 1837 a pop. of 176,570. There are 17 towns in the co., each of which is the capital of the district in which it stands. The territory is intersected by the Isonzo, which, with its tributary streams, contributes much to the fertility of the soil. The celebrated Timavus is also included in this district. It comprises every variety of soil and surface. GOR The portion which it embraces of the Carso, as well as other parts bordering on Carniola, is barren. Other mountainous districts are covered with forests of towering pines and other valuable timber. The seacoast about Aquileia is a pestilential marsh; while the plains, the valleys, and the hills on the side of Italy are exceedingly fertile, the soil and cultivation being but little inferior to the most favoured spots in Lombardy. The surface is equally diversified. To the N the land rises by gradations, from hills covered with vines to woody mountains, and Alps of the elevation of 9,000 to 10,000 ft. To the S are bare hills which overlook the Adriatic. To the E is a lofty but fruitful valley leading to the higher regions of Carniola, bounded by Mount Nanos; and to the W lie the beautiful plains which connect it with Italy. There is likewise great variety in the inhabitants in their origin, language, and habits. One moiety, that to the E and S, is Slavonic; the other Roman-Gothic; both meet in the city of G., each preserving its distinctive marks of language, features and dress. The merely political union of lands and people so different has not altered the local laws or customs with regard to landed property, dominion, vassalage or servitude, or affected the tenures or conditions by which land is occupied and cultivated, which are in fact as various as the face of the country or the nature of the soil. There are some extensive fiefs belonging to noble families, the subjects of which are little better than serfs; there are also a great number of petty proprietors, free or copyholders, similar to those in the districts of Trieste and Istria; but the most important part of the country belongs to a middle class of proprietors, distinct from the higher nobility, and whose estates are cultivated under their own inspection, by farmers who retain the ancient denomination of coloni. These operative farmers have generally the use of the buildings and stock, for which they pay a small consideration as rent. The wine belongs exclusively to the proprietors; and the occupier is remunerated by a portion of the remaining produce. chiefly maize, and sometimes the profit of the farmyard. Trieste is a good market for their superfluous produce, wine, grain, geese, turkeys, poultry, eggs, fruits, vegetables, &c.; and also affords employment to numbers of the redundant male and female population. The condition of the peasantry in G. is in general better than that of Istria, though perhaps not equal to that of the free cultivators of Trieste. They are well clothed; and their habitations, though rude, are solid. None of the native inhabitants of these provs. pay much attention to comfort or neatness in their dwellings or gardens; there is nothing like a Swiss or English cottage in these parts. The Slavonic tribes are more laborious than the Furlains, as they are called, or Roman-Gothic race; but the latter are more sprightly and ingenious. Both races are hardy and healthy, and make good soldiers.—The cap., of the same name, stands on the l. bank of the Isonzo, 22 m. NNW of Trieste, in N lat. 45° 571. Pop. in 1838, 8,252. It is divided into an upper and lower town. The former is situated on a mountain, and defended by a castle; the latter stands on a plain adjacent to the river. Its chief manufactures are leather, silk, ribbons, shirtings, and rosoglio. There is an extensive sugar-refinery here. In 1751 an archbishop's see was erected here, but it was suppressed in 1782. The cathedral is a fine building. In 1797 G. was taken by the French. The castle-in which Charles X., ex-king of France, died in 1836, commands a delightful prospect; but is described by M. de la Rochefoucauld as "a small house, seated desolately on a hill, and looking at most like the abode of some citizen's family."
GORITZ, a small town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, circle of Lebus, on the Oder, 7 m. S of Custrin. Pop. 1,750.

GORITZA, a village of Turkey, 3 m. E of Volo, in the sanjak of Tricala, on the site of the ancient Demetrias. It was a place of great importance under the last kings of Macedon, who made it their residence, and considered it the key of Greece.-Also a village in the sanjak of Joannina, on the slope of Mount Lazares.
GORKHA. See GORCHA.
GORKUM. See GORCUM.
GORK-SOLENOE, a lake of Russia, in the gov. of Saratof, 60 m. SE of Kamoitchen. It is 12 m. in length from SSE to NNW, and about 5 m. in greatest breadth.
GORLAGO, a village of Austrian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 7 m. ESE of Bergamo. Pop. 1,200.

GORLESTON, a parish and village of Suffolk, 2 m. S of Great Yarmouth, and included within the parl. boundaries of that borough. Pop. in 1831, 2,116; in 1841, 2,355. The v. is connected with Yarmouth by a bridge over the Bredon.
GORLICE, a town of Austrian Poland, in the circle of Jaslo, 20 m. ENE of Neu Sandec. Pop. 2,547.
GORLINGEN, a large village of Wurtemberg, in the bail. of Leonberg.

GORLITZ, a town of Prussian Silesia, in Upper Lusatia, on the l. bank of the Neisse, at an alt. of 310 ft. above sea-level; and on the Saxon-Silesian railway from Dresden to G. and Zittau, by which it is 633 m. from Dresden, 29 m. from Bautzen, and 21 m. from Zittau. It had a pop. of 13,670 in 1837; 15.230 in 1846. It is tolerably well-built and walled; and has long been noted for its woollen and linen manufactures. It has also steel and iron-works, bleaching establishments, and a sugar-refinery. G. has a great provincial school, founded in 1565, a library of 20,000 vols., and a cabinet of natural history. It contains 8 churches, one of which has an excellent organ, and one has a spire of great height. It has 4 hospitals, and an orphan-asylum. The town has been frequently injured by fires in the 17th and 18th cents.; and has also suffered severely from the sieges to which it has been exposed. The battle of Bautzen (21st May 1813) was brought to a close in the neighbourhood of this town.
GORLIZ, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 13 m. NNE of Bilbao, on the bay of Biseay. Pop. 1,000.
GORMANSTON, a village of co. Meath, 2 m. NW of Balbriggan. Pop. 160.
GORMAZ, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 34 m. SSW of Soria. Pop. 200.
GORNIKI, a village of Prussia, in the reg. of Oppeln, circle of Beuthen.
GORNOSTAIPOL, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 43 m. NNW of Kiev, on the l. bank of the Teterev. Pop. 500.
GORO, or Po-DI-GORO, a branch of the river Po, which falls into the gulf of Venice, in a marshy tract containing 3 villages, called G., Mezzo-G., and Codigoro.

GOROBLAGODAT, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Perm, N of Tagilsk. There are large iron-mines in the vicinity, in the Blagodat, a rich magnetic mountain, which rises about 450 ft. above the plain of the Kushva.
GOROCHOVITZ. See GOREKHOF.
GORODETZ, a small town of Russia, in the gov. of Grodno, 10 m. E of Kobrin.
GORODILOVO, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 66 m. ESE of Vilna.
GORODIN, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 92 m. W of Poltava, on the l. bank of the Soula.

GORODISCHITSH, a town of Russia, in the gov. S of Samenov. Pop. 3,000, employed chiefly in the printing of linen, the preparation of dye-stuffs, and of white lead.—Also a town in the gov. of Grod no, 24 m. SSE of Novogrodek .- Also a small town in the gov. of Kiev, on the Dnieper, 112 m. SE of Kiev.—Also a town in the gov. of Penza, 32 m. ESE of Penza. Pop. 2,000.— Also a village in the gov. of Voronetz, 36 m. ESE of Starobielsk.—Also a town in the gov. and 95 m. WNW of Poltava.—Also a village in the gov. of Tobolsk, 20 m. ESE of Jen iceysk.
GORODNIA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Chernigov, 35 m. N of Chernigov. Pop. 1,700.
GORODNITZA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, 21 m. NW of Novgrad-Velinsk.

GORODOK, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Minsk, 24 m. WSW of Bobrisk.—Also a town of Russia, 67 m. E of Polotsk, on a small river of the same name, in a district remarkable for its numerous small lakes.
GORODOK-BORISCIV, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Moscow, 6 m. S of Mojaisk, on the l. bank of the Protva.
GORODOK-SAKMA RSK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 22 m. N of Oremburg, on the r. bank of the Sakmara.
GOROMUR, a district of Assam, in the division of Majuli, to the E of I)eanpat. It is about 40 m. in length, and 15 m. in breadth.
GORON, a small towre of France, in the dep. and 9 m. NW of Mayenne. Pop. 2,228. Pop. of cant., comprising 11 com., 14,181.
GORONGO, a small island in the Eastern seas, 25 m. SE of Gilolo, in S lat. 1° 8'.
GORRA. See SUTLEDGE.
GORRAN (SAINT), a parish and village of Cornwall, 5 m. ESE of Tregoney. Area 4,660 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,205; in 1841, 1,332.
GORREDYK, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, 18 m. SE of Leeuwarden. Pop. 1,700.
GORREVOD, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Pont-de-Vaux, 20 m. NW of Bourgen-Bresse. Pop. 1,736.
GORRITI, an island of the Rio-de-la-Plata, in S lat. 34° 56'.
GORSA, a small island near the S coast of Cuba, in N lat. 21° 45'.
GORSCHEN, a village of Prussian Saxony, in the reg. and 12 m. SE of Merseburg. The battle of Lutzen, in 1813, was fought near this v.
GORSES, a town of France, in the dep. of Lot, 12 m. N of Figeac. Pop. 1,174.
GORSK, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Cheringof, 15 m. NE of Gorodnia.
GORSZKOW, a small town of Poland, in the palatinate of Lublin, 18 m. NNW of Zamosk.
GORT, a neat town of co. Galway, 15 m. N by E of Ennis. It has a barrack, a market-house, a district bridewell, and a poor-law workhouse. Pop. in 1831, 3,627; in 1841, 3,056.
GORTEEN, a village in the p. of Kilfree, co. Sligo. Pop. 177.
GORTIN, a village in the p. of Lower Badony, co. Tyrone. Pop. 410.
GORTON, a chapelry in the p. and 4 m. SE of Manchester. Pop. in 1831, 2,623; in 1841, 2,422.
GORTROE, a parish in co. Cork, 2 m. SSE of Ratheormac. Area 9,043 acres. Pop. 2,832.
